ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sweet biscuits and cookies broadly; no Scotch finger-specific code exists. Used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Scotch finger biscuit or shortbread biscuit category as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Scotch finger biscuits is 2 biscuits (approximately 25 g), providing around 125 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Scotch Finger Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080060 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — shortbread biscuit entry; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database shortbread/butter biscuit category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to 'Scotch finger biscuit' is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 64 is taken from the closest analogous entry — plain shortbread / butter biscuit — as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI table (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 64 × 16.19 / 100 ≈ 10.4 (based on 25 g serving containing 16.19 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (419 mg/100 g) combined with high sugar (26.43 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Scotch finger biscuits should be avoided by individuals with hypertension: sodium at 419 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold, and the high sugar load (26.43 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (26.43 g/100 g), high refined carbohydrate (64.75 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and medium-high GI of 64
type2_diabetes.guidance: Scotch finger biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes: sugar at 26.43 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, refined carbohydrates total 64.75 g/100 g with zero fibre to moderate absorption, and the GI of 64 produces a glycaemic load of approximately 10.4 per 25 g serving, causing rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.34 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~11.5 g/100 g, high sodium (419 mg/100 g), and high sugar (26.43 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Scotch finger biscuits pose a high cardiovascular risk: estimated saturated fat of approximately 11.5 g/100 g from butter raises LDL cholesterol, sodium at 419 mg/100 g contributes to hypertensive cardiovascular load, and the high sugar content promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation; regular consumption should be avoided in individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scotch finger biscuit is a rectangular, crisp shortbread-style biscuit of Australian origin, widely consumed across Australia, New Zealand, and Singapore. It is made from wheat flour, butter, sugar, milk solids, and salt, baked until golden and lightly crisp with a characteristic snap. The texture is firm and crumbly, with a rich buttery flavour and a moderately sweet taste from the sugar coating. Scotch finger biscuits are ultra-processed, high in refined carbohydrates (64.75 g/100 g), total fat (23.34 g/100 g), and sugar (26.43 g/100 g), with significant sodium (419 mg/100 g) and zero dietary fibre. The estimated saturated fat content of approximately 11.5 g/100 g from butter makes them a high-saturated-fat food. Clinically, they are unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to their high glycaemic load, sodium content, and saturated fat profile. They are commonly consumed as a snack or with tea or coffee. The most recognised commercial brand in Australia is Arnott's Scotch Finger. A typical serving is 2 biscuits (approximately 25 g), providing around 125 kcal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080060, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest shortbread/butter biscuit category entry; confidence for GI component is medium but does not reduce overall record confidence below high given the strong macro provenance.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — shortbread biscuits contain negligible dietary fibre by formulation. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the source record. saturated_fat_g of 11.5 g/100 g is estimated from the known butter/shortening composition of shortbread biscuits and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central entry for shortbread cookies (NDB 18158); this field is not explicitly reported in the HPB record. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB Scotch finger record are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database shortbread/butter biscuit category; no exact Scotch finger GI entry exists in the database. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural record appears inconsistent with the ingredient profile of a standard shortbread biscuit (refined wheat flour); this tag has not been carried forward as it is unsupported by the source.
